chapters.title: The natural desire to be rebellious
chapters.text:
Children get to a certain age where they become stubborn, and it is at this point that parents must put in more effort to teach them good habits. Although most of their actions can be attributed to age, some children suffer from severe psychological and behavioral problems such as language development, socialization, hostility, irritability, excessive worrying or unusual anxiety, extreme rudeness, etc. But all of these issues must be addressed because it becomes more difficult for children to change once they’re adults.

Parenting is not easy, particularly for Christian parents. Children nowadays are more exposed to the world’s ills and many negative influences that would make it difficult for them to do things in the Lord’s way.

Living with children can be frustrating, but learning and adopting better parenting techniques will make parenthood fun.

One essential thing that should be noted is that you can't give kids everything they want. It would be best to make them understand that life is not a bed of roses. This means you have to nurture and support your children regularly so that they can know more about life.

In this summary, you learn three basic steps that you can use for effective parenting:

• Controlling obnoxious behavior
• Encouraging good behavior
• Maintaining a healthy relationship with your children

Let’s get started!

chapters.title: Determining the cause of your child's frustration
chapters.text:
There are two frustrating behaviors that kids exhibit:

• The Start behavior
• The Stop behavior

Stop behaviors include the constant, everyday issues kids present to you. These behaviors include whining, gross disrespect, throwing tantrums, etc. Although these are minor issues, they can cause a very frustrating day when you add them together.

On the other hand, Start behaviors include positive activities like doing homework, getting up early, doing chores, cleaning the room, etc. These behaviors can be more difficult than the Stop behaviors because the issue is with what the kids are not doing.

Identifying the different behaviors is essential to knowing which habit-creation technique to use for your kids.

The difference between the two behaviors is in the issue of motivation. You can easily motivate a child to stop a behavior, but it’s more difficult to motivate a child to exhibit a Start behavior. For the Stop behaviors such as teasing or arguing, yelling, throwing tantrums, etc., you can use the 1-2-3 or counting procedure. This procedure involves counting to a particular number as a timer for your child to stop a particular behavior.

For the Start behavior such as doing homework, cleaning rooms, and getting up early, you can use some techniques such as simple requests, natural consequences, praise, etc.

The inability to accept a mistake is an obnoxious behavior in children, mainly exhibited with Stop behaviors. This means that to stop obnoxious behavior, you must employ the 1-2-3 or counting procedure.

For example, when your child starts throwing tantrums, you count to three. On the first two counts, you've given a child the opportunity to shape up. However, if the child fails to compose, there will be a consequence. The consequence can be a timeout or a rest period. Time out or rest is great for kids because it gives them time to self-reflect on their actions.

The advantage of this procedure is that you can easily control your child's behavior from wherever you are. It also shows that your authority is not negotiable, and you can rest assured that you are not too harsh or gentle.

Did you know? According to the Pew Research Center, two-thirds of parents in the U.S. believe parenting is harder today than it was two decades ago, with many citing technology as a reason.

chapters.title: Getting started with the 1-2-3 procedure
chapters.text:
The 1-2-3, or counting, procedure starts with the Kickoff Conversation, which is the stage where you explain the meaning of the procedure and the consequences of disobedience to your child. Then, tell them what they should be doing and what you expect from them. Most children don't understand the 1-2-3 counting method until you've done it a few times. So, you have to give them the benefit of the doubt to grasp the concept better. The method might seem ineffective initially, but once you continue applying it, your child will come around, and you'll see visible changes.

Both parents should preferably do the Kickoff Conversation. This gives the child the impression that it's a serious issue and that there'll hardly be any compromise from either parent.

If you fail to stand your ground when parenting a child, you'll find initiating good behavior or triggering positive habits difficult.

Both you and your partner should gather your children and ask them to sit before you. Explain that you're implementing a new approach: if they do something they shouldn't, you'll say, “That's 1,” as a warning to stop. If they persist, you'll say, “That's 2,” which is the second warning. If they continue, you'll say, “That's 3, take 5.”

Using this example, clarify that “take 5” means a timeout in their room for a rest period. After the timeout, avoid discussing what happened unless necessary. Instead, move forward and start anew.

Children can’t respond properly to warnings if they don’t hear them clearly in the first place. ~ Thomas W. Phelan, PhD

It's important to understand that children may not always express gratitude or show immediate understanding for your efforts to raise them responsibly. Just get going, stick to your methods, and always remember to count.

chapters.title: Any parent’s biggest nightmare
chapters.text:
Many kids love to make their parents feel embarrassed in public because there's a limit to the level of punishment parents can throw out on a child in public view. Many parents are unsure how to control their kids in public, especially in a crowded place where people will most likely side with the child. The problem with public places is that kids can use them as leverage because they know you'd fear public embarrassment.

When faced with the possibility of public embarrassment, all you need to do is bring out your 1-2-3 procedure and apply it again. Be calm and firm when counting. Avoid being intimidated by the threat of public embarrassment, and stop making the child feel that they have power over you. Make them realize that you do not care what others think and provide a timeout place to send the child. For example, you can use the restrooms if you're in a mall.

Avoid nagging or complaining when correcting your child publicly to prevent increased rebellion.

Make the child know that you're in control of the situation and won't compromise, irrespective of the level of tantrums thrown.

You can take an erring kid to the car and instruct them to sit for five or 10 minutes without saying a word. However, it should be noted that leaving a child alone inside the car is not advisable. Another way to get the youngster to cooperate is by making the child understand that you would never come to public places with them if they fail to cooperate.

chapters.title: Children are not always grateful
chapters.text:
Although children comprehend the significance of parenting, they occasionally resort to testing and manipulation. These behaviors serve as tactics frustrated kids use to get what they want and evade punishment. There are three things every Christian parent must understand about testing:

1. Testing happens when a child is frustrated, and they will test your resolve.
2. Testing is purposeful behavior because they don’t want you to impose your will onto them.
3. There are six tactics involved during testing and manipulation:

• Badgering is when a child repeatedly begs or nags till you give in to their demands.
• Temper (Intimidation). Toddlers or young children who may struggle with expressing themselves verbally might resort to throwing themselves on the floor, hitting their heads on furniture, or screaming.
• Threat. Frustrated kids will most likely threaten their parents with bad things if they don’t get what they want.
• Martyrdom. Using this tactic, the child will opt for manipulation by indicating that their life has become unfair and an incredible burden.
• Butter Up. Instead of making you feel embarrassed and angry, the child tries to make you feel good about a decision that you’re not entirely comfortable with.
• Physical Tactics. An upset child might hit an adult, break things, or try to run away from home.

Several things are important when faced with any of these tactics: Don't get discouraged, count when necessary, and keep quiet, except when you need to give necessary explanations.

Be patient as your child adjusts to new behaviors, maintaining your position without compromise.

Over time, these tactics will diminish, and your children will become more accepting of your discipline without causing significant issues when they feel frustrated.

chapters.title: Cultivate the habit of praising your child
chapters.text:
As a Christian parent, you should endeavor to improve your relationship with your children through regular doses of praise, personal shared fun, a listening ear, and constant affection. Doing this will make them feel appreciated for adopting good behavior. However, it is important to note that you must have a solid personal relationship with your children before praising them. This is where one-on-one shared fun comes in.

Find enough time to spend with your children, teach them the ways of the Lord, and be a good role model for them.

Another key aspect to consider involves implementing the Major or Minor System. This structured approach outlines the repercussions of negative behaviors, with consequences ranging from major offenses to minor infractions. Alternatively, based on the severity of the behavior, you can establish a tiered system of Major, Medium, and Minor consequences, such as fines, chores, or educational tasks.

For instance:

Major Consequences

• Two-week grounding to their room
• Fine of $20 or double the value of stolen or damaged items
• 14 hours of household chores
• 14 hours of community service or educational activities

Medium Consequences

• One-week grounding after dinner
• Fine of $8 or double the value of stolen/damaged items
• Five hours of household chores
• Five hours of community service or educational tasks

Minor Consequences

• Two-day grounding after dinner with no electronic entertainment or phone usage
• Smaller fines
• Two hours of household work
• Two hours of community service
• Educational activities

Once you have established your punishment classifications, you can decide which behavior deserves which punishment class. When your child errs, one of the consequences from the list you’ve created will be implemented. This approach not only saves time and effort but also provides your child with prior knowledge of the consequences for negative behaviors.

chapters.title: Substituting bad behavior
chapters.text:
You can trigger positive habits by encouraging your children to do the positive things you would like them to do. For example, in the Start Behaviors mentioned in the previous chapters, tasks like doing homework, getting up early, cleaning rooms, etc., require more motivation. While it might take only five seconds to stop a kid from throwing tantrums, telling a kid to start a task, such as doing homework or getting up early, will require 20 minutes or more. Start Behaviors require more motivation because you have to help the kids sustain the behavior.

To trigger good habits in your children's lives, you must lead by example.

They not only have to start these tasks, but they also have to continue and finish them.

There are seven approaches to promoting good behavior:

1. Positive reinforcement involves reminding your child that having positive behaviors is the way of the Lord.
2. Simple requests motivate your kids to do what they're supposed to.
3. Kitchen timers are good devices to encourage kids to finish their tasks for a reward.
4. The docking system enforces that they won't get paid if they don't do the work.
5. Natural consequences are needed to enforce change.
6. Charting is an amicable method that uses a calendar to track how well a child is doing.
7. Counting [different version] requires counting for longer periods. You can count using minutes or hours instead of using the 1-2-3 method.

chapters.title: Encourage your child’s growing independence
chapters.text:
Now that you’ve stopped obnoxious behaviors, encouraged good ones, and maintained healthy relationships, it’s time to strengthen your relationship with your child. This involves taking a realistic look at their self-esteem. The bond you share with your child directly impacts their self-esteem. Strengthening this relationship will invariably boost your child's confidence and self-worth.

A healthy self-esteem can be created using the following elements:

• An unconditional love of God
• Good and healthy relationships with others
• Competence and diligence in work
• Self-love, practical skills, and caring for your body
• Building character through courage, effort, and concern for others

It’s easy to mistake healthy self-esteem for over-parenting. If you happen to be the type who worries unnecessarily, you should learn to keep some of your worries to yourself.

You shouldn’t force your kids to love God; instead, give them enough reasons, through your actions, to do it.

Strive to engage in activities together as a family, ensuring these moments are enjoyable for everyone involved. Being a Christian doesn’t mean you have to do away with anything fun. There are many Christian groups you can join and many Christian books you can share with your family.

At the end of the day, the most overwhelming key to a child's success is the positive involvement of parents. ~ Jane D. Hull

A good parent is an active listener. You can know if you are an active listener if you are focused on understanding what your child is saying when talking to you. After God, you are your children’s haven. Always pray for God’s guidance over them.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Many parents find it difficult to teach their kids how to show good behavior. This can be especially difficult for Christian parents since many distractions are open for their kids. A good Christian parent should teach their child the ways of the Almighty Lord. They must be encouraged to have Christ-like behaviors, including love for others, obedience to parents, regard for elders, love for God, and a caring heart. These can be difficult to achieve if you don't know how to parent a child.

The first step to helping a child cultivate good habits and behavior is determining if the child is suffering from the Stop or the Start Behaviors. Once this has been established, you can proceed to the next step using the techniques discussed in the previous chapters. You'll not find the desired results if you use the Stop techniques for the Start Behaviors. Every kid is unique, so you can't expect them to react to the methods discussed in this summary the same way. Good parenting involves being patient with your kid enough to teach them what is needed to give them a Christian life.

The 1-2-3 procedure is an effective way of getting your children to become the best versions of themselves. What’s beautiful about these procedures is that they are easily practicable, and the results are easily visible. Always remember to be a good person and an ideal role model for your kids. If you fail to exhibit good behaviors, you’ll find it difficult to get them to listen to you.

Try this

Organize a weekly session with your children and discuss the characters of the good people in the Bible. Tell them about the heavenly rewards of waiting for a good person, and teach them how to follow in the footsteps of Christ.

Read it till the end?
